title: Adrenal Crisis
objectives: Recognize clinical and laboratory features of adrenal crisis
Distinguish primary from secondary adrenal insufficiency
Initiate immediate treatment without delaying for diagnostic confirmation
Apply stress dosing rules for patients with known adrenal insufficiency
Identify precipitating factors for adrenal crisis
Educate patients on sick day rules and emergency management

guidelineReferences.name: Endocrine Society: Primary Adrenal Insufficiency Guideline
guidelineReferences.citation: Bornstein SR, et al. Diagnosis and Treatment of Primary Adrenal Insufficiency: An Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline. J Clin Endocrinol Metab. 2016;101(2):364-389
keyPoints: Treat immediately with IV hydrocortisone 100mg before diagnostic tests
Follow with 200mg hydrocortisone/24h (continuous infusion or 50mg q6h)
Aggressive fluid resuscitation with normal saline

guidelineReferences.name: Society for Endocrinology: Adrenal Crisis Emergency Guidance
guidelineReferences.citation: Society for Endocrinology. Emergency Management of Acute Adrenal Insufficiency. Endocrine Connections. 2016;5(5):G1-G3

guidelineReferences.name: StatPearls: Adrenal Crisis
guidelineReferences.citation: Nicolaides NC, et al. Adrenal Crisis. StatPearls [Internet]. 2025 Feb 15

vignette.text:
A 54-year-old man is brought to the ED by his wife after being found confused at home. He has a history of autoimmune adrenal insufficiency (Addison's disease) diagnosed 5 years ago and takes hydrocortisone and fludrocortisone. His wife reports he developed gastroenteritis 2 days ago with vomiting and diarrhea and has been unable to keep his medications down. He became progressively weak, confused, and lethargic over the past 12 hours.

Past Medical History: Addison's disease (5 years), Autoimmune thyroiditis (on levothyroxine), Type 1 diabetes mellitus

Medications: Hydrocortisone 20mg AM / 10mg PM, Fludrocortisone 0.1mg daily, Levothyroxine 75mcg daily, Insulin glargine and lispro

Allergies: None

Social History: Non-smoker, accountant

physicalExam.general: Lethargic, confused, ill-appearing, hyperpigmented skin creases and buccal mucosa
physicalExam.cardiovascular: Tachycardic, regular, weak pulses, delayed capillary refill
physicalExam.skin: Hyperpigmentation of palmar creases, gingiva, and recent scars; poor skin turgor

questions.stem: This patient has hypotension, confusion, hyponatremia, hyperkalemia, hypoglycemia, and hyperpigmentation.
questions.question: What is the classic presentation of adrenal crisis and why is immediate recognition critical?
questions.hint: Consider the constellation of findings and mortality implications.

options.text: Diagnosis requires ACTH stimulation test before treatment
Hypotension/shock refractory to fluids, hyponatremia, hyperkalemia, hypoglycemia, abdominal pain, altered mental status; mortality 8-25% if untreated, immediate hydrocortisone is life-saving
Only hypoglycemia is concerning
Hypertension and hypokalemia

questions.explanation: Adrenal crisis presents with: Cardiovascular collapse (hypotension refractory to fluids/vasopressors), Electrolyte abnormalities (hyponatremia from cortisol deficiency + hyperkalemia from aldosterone deficiency in primary AI), Hypoglycemia (cortisol is counter-regulatory), GI symptoms (nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain), Altered mental status. Mortality is 8-25% even with treatment, making immediate recognition and hydrocortisone administration life-saving.

whyChain.why: Why is hypotension refractory to fluids alone?
whyChain.answer: Cortisol is essential for vascular tone and catecholamine responsiveness. Without cortisol, blood vessels cannot constrict appropriately, and the heart cannot respond to catecholamines. Fluids and vasopressors will not work until glucocorticoids are given.

In PRIMARY adrenal insufficiency, loss of cortisol feedback causes elevated ACTH. ACTH shares a precursor (POMC) with melanocyte-stimulating hormone (MSH). Elevated ACTH/MSH causes hyperpigmentation in skin creases, scars, gingiva, and buccal mucosa. Secondary AI does NOT cause hyperpigmentation (ACTH is low).

sources.text: StatPearls: Adrenal Crisis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: The patient has Addison's disease (primary adrenal insufficiency) with known autoimmune thyroiditis and type 1 diabetes.
questions.question: What syndrome does this constellation represent and why does it increase crisis risk?

options.text: MEN Type 1
Isolated Addison's disease
Autoimmune Polyglandular Syndrome Type 2 (Schmidt syndrome): autoimmune adrenalitis + autoimmune thyroid disease ± type 1 diabetes; multiple hormone deficiencies increase crisis risk
Secondary adrenal insufficiency

questions.explanation: Autoimmune Polyglandular Syndrome Type 2 (APS-2/Schmidt syndrome): Autoimmune adrenalitis (Addison's) + Autoimmune thyroiditis (Hashimoto's or Graves') ± Type 1 diabetes. Most common cause of primary AI in developed countries. Multiple endocrine deficiencies complicate management - thyroid dysfunction can alter cortisol metabolism, and diabetes adds hypoglycemia risk.

whyChain.why: How does thyroid status affect adrenal crisis?
whyChain.answer: Hypothyroidism decreases cortisol clearance (may mask AI), while hyperthyroidism increases clearance (may precipitate crisis). Always assess thyroid status in AI patients. TSH may be elevated in untreated AI due to loss of cortisol's inhibitory effect - don't start thyroid hormone until cortisol is replaced.
APS-1 (APECED): childhood onset, caused by AIRE gene mutations. Triad of chronic mucocutaneous candidiasis, hypoparathyroidism, and adrenal insufficiency. Less common than APS-2.

sources.text: StatPearls: Adrenal Crisis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: The patient has hyponatremia (124) and hyperkalemia (6.2).
questions.question: Why does PRIMARY adrenal insufficiency cause both hyponatremia AND hyperkalemia?
questions.hint: Consider which hormones are deficient in primary vs secondary AI.

options.text: Secondary AI causes the same electrolyte pattern
Only cortisol deficiency causes electrolyte abnormalities

Primary AI causes BOTH cortisol AND aldosterone deficiency; cortisol deficiency → hyponatremia (increased ADH, decreased free water excretion); aldosterone deficiency → hyperkalemia and sodium wasting
Electrolyte abnormalities are due to vomiting only

questions.explanation: Primary AI = adrenal gland destruction → deficiency of BOTH cortisol (glucocorticoid) AND aldosterone (mineralocorticoid). Cortisol deficiency: ↑ADH release → water retention → dilutional hyponatremia. Aldosterone deficiency: ↓Na reabsorption + ↓K excretion in distal nephron → sodium wasting + hyperkalemia. Secondary AI (pituitary) only affects cortisol (ACTH-dependent) - aldosterone is preserved (regulated by renin-angiotensin), so hyperkalemia does NOT occur.

whyChain.why: Why is the electrolyte pattern important diagnostically?
whyChain.answer: Hyperkalemia distinguishes PRIMARY from SECONDARY AI. Primary AI = hyponatremia + hyperkalemia. Secondary AI = hyponatremia only (aldosterone intact). This helps localize the lesion before formal testing.

whyChain.why: What causes hypercalcemia in adrenal crisis?
whyChain.answer: Cortisol normally inhibits intestinal calcium absorption and increases renal calcium excretion. Cortisol deficiency → increased calcium absorption + decreased excretion → hypercalcemia. Usually mild but can contribute to symptoms.

sources.text: StatPearls: Adrenal Crisis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

Bornstein SR, et al. Diagnosis and Treatment of Primary Adrenal Insufficiency: An Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline. J Clin Endocrinol Metab. 2016;101(2):364-389. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.question: Why is eosinophilia a clue to adrenal insufficiency?

options.text: Eosinophilia is unrelated to adrenal function
Eosinophilia indicates allergic reaction to medications
Eosinophilia indicates parasitic infection
Cortisol normally suppresses eosinophils; in stress, cortisol rises and eosinophils fall; normal/elevated eosinophils in a stressed patient suggests cortisol deficiency

questions.explanation: Cortisol induces eosinophil apoptosis and sequestration. During stress, cortisol rises and eosinophils typically fall (<1%). Finding NORMAL or ELEVATED eosinophils in an acutely stressed/ill patient is paradoxical and should raise suspicion for adrenal insufficiency. This patient's 12% eosinophilia in the setting of sepsis-like presentation is a red flag.

whyChain.why: What other CBC findings suggest adrenal insufficiency?
whyChain.answer: Lymphocytosis may also occur (cortisol normally causes lymphopenia). Anemia (normocytic) can develop chronically. The absence of expected stress leukocytosis despite infection/stress should raise AI suspicion.

sources.text: StatPearls: Adrenal Crisis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

Bornstein SR, et al. Diagnosis and Treatment of Primary Adrenal Insufficiency: An Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline. J Clin Endocrinol Metab. 2016;101(2):364-389. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: This patient developed gastroenteritis and could not keep his oral medications down.
questions.question: What are the common precipitants of adrenal crisis in patients with known adrenal insufficiency?
questions.hint: Consider what increases cortisol demand or prevents replacement.

options.text: Adrenal crisis cannot occur in patients on replacement therapy
Taking too much hydrocortisone
Only surgical procedures
Infection (most common), GI illness preventing oral medication absorption, surgery/trauma, medication non-compliance, physiologic stress (pregnancy, severe emotional stress)

questions.explanation: Adrenal crisis occurs when cortisol supply cannot meet demand. Precipitants: (1) Infection - most common, increases cortisol requirement; (2) GI illness - vomiting/diarrhea prevents oral medication absorption (this patient); (3) Surgery/trauma/procedures - increased stress demand; (4) Non-compliance - stopping medications; (5) Inadequate stress dosing during illness. Incidence: ~8 per 100 patient-years in those with AI.

whyChain.why: Why didn't the patient self-inject hydrocortisone?
whyChain.answer: This is a critical failure point. All AI patients should have emergency injectable hydrocortisone at home and training on self/family administration. When unable to take oral medications, IM hydrocortisone should be given immediately. This patient likely lacked this or didn't act quickly enough.

whyChain.why: What about patients who DON'T know they have AI?
whyChain.answer: Approximately half of adrenal crises occur as the FIRST presentation of previously undiagnosed adrenal insufficiency. These patients are at highest risk because there's no warning or preparation. Maintain high clinical suspicion in unexplained shock.

sources.text: StatPearls: Adrenal Crisis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: You suspect adrenal crisis in this hypotensive patient.
questions.question: What is the IMMEDIATE treatment and why should it NEVER be delayed for diagnostic testing?
questions.hint: Consider the life-saving nature of hydrocortisone.

options.text: Give dexamethasone as first choice
IV Hydrocortisone 100mg bolus immediately, then 200mg/24h continuous infusion or 50mg IV q6h; NEVER delay for cortisol/ACTH levels - treatment is life-saving with no harm from single dose
Wait for cortisol level to confirm diagnosis
Start with oral hydrocortisone

questions.explanation: IMMEDIATE treatment: (1) IV Hydrocortisone 100mg bolus - this is the FIRST action; (2) Follow with 200mg/24h (continuous infusion preferred, or 50mg q6h); (3) Aggressive IV fluids (NS initially); (4) Correct hypoglycemia with dextrose. NEVER delay treatment for diagnosis - a single dose of hydrocortisone has no adverse effects, but untreated adrenal crisis is rapidly fatal. Blood can be drawn for cortisol/ACTH before hydrocortisone if it doesn't delay treatment.

whyChain.answer: Hydrocortisone provides both glucocorticoid AND mineralocorticoid activity at stress doses (>50mg/day has significant mineralocorticoid effect). This addresses both cortisol and aldosterone deficiency. Prednisolone is alternative; dexamethasone is least preferred (no mineralocorticoid activity).

whyChain.why: Can we draw cortisol before treatment?
whyChain.answer: Yes, IF it causes no delay. Random cortisol <3 μg/dL in stressed patient is diagnostic. Cortisol 3-15 μg/dL is indeterminate. >18-20 μg/dL in stressed patient suggests intact adrenal function. ACTH drawn simultaneously helps distinguish primary (high ACTH) from secondary (low ACTH) AI.

sources.text: StatPearls: Adrenal Crisis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

Bornstein SR, et al. Diagnosis and Treatment of Primary Adrenal Insufficiency: An Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline. J Clin Endocrinol Metab. 2016;101(2):364-389. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: The patient has hyperkalemia (6.2) with peaked T waves on ECG.
questions.question: How should hyperkalemia be managed in adrenal crisis?
questions.hint: Consider what corrects the underlying cause.

options.text: Hydrocortisone itself helps correct hyperkalemia; standard hyperkalemia treatment (calcium gluconate, insulin/dextrose, etc.) if severe/symptomatic; avoid potassium in IV fluids
Emergent dialysis is always required
Give potassium supplements
Hyperkalemia cannot be treated until AI is resolved

questions.explanation: Hyperkalemia management: (1) Hydrocortisone replaces mineralocorticoid effect and will help correct hyperkalemia over hours; (2) For severe hyperkalemia (>6.5 or ECG changes): Calcium gluconate for cardiac protection, Insulin + dextrose, consider albuterol, kayexalate/patiromer if needed; (3) Use normal saline WITHOUT potassium for fluid resuscitation. Hyperkalemia often corrects with hydrocortisone and fluids alone.

whyChain.answer: At stress doses (>50mg/day), hydrocortisone has enough mineralocorticoid activity. Fludrocortisone is NOT needed acutely. Once hydrocortisone is tapered below 50mg/day, fludrocortisone (typically 0.05-0.1mg daily) should be added for patients with primary AI.

sources.text: StatPearls: Adrenal Crisis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

Bornstein SR, et al. Diagnosis and Treatment of Primary Adrenal Insufficiency: An Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline. J Clin Endocrinol Metab. 2016;101(2):364-389. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.question: Why does hypoglycemia occur in adrenal crisis and how should it be managed?
questions.hint: Consider cortisol's role in glucose metabolism.

options.text: Hypoglycemia only occurs in children
Avoid dextrose to prevent hyperglycemia
Cortisol is a counter-regulatory hormone that promotes gluconeogenesis; deficiency causes hypoglycemia; treat with IV dextrose and monitor closely, especially in diabetics
Hypoglycemia is coincidental

questions.explanation: Cortisol is a counter-regulatory hormone - it increases gluconeogenesis, decreases peripheral glucose uptake, and antagonizes insulin. Cortisol deficiency → reduced gluconeogenesis + increased insulin sensitivity → hypoglycemia. This patient also has Type 1 DM, increasing risk. Treatment: IV dextrose (D10 or D50), add dextrose to maintenance fluids, monitor glucose frequently until stable.

whyChain.why: How should this diabetic patient's insulin be managed?
whyChain.answer: Hold or significantly reduce insulin doses during acute crisis. The patient has both hypoglycemia risk from AI AND underlying diabetes. Once stable on hydrocortisone (which raises glucose), insulin requirements will increase. Close glucose monitoring and individualized insulin dosing are essential.

sources.text: StatPearls: Adrenal Crisis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

Bornstein SR, et al. Diagnosis and Treatment of Primary Adrenal Insufficiency: An Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline. J Clin Endocrinol Metab. 2016;101(2):364-389. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: A different patient on chronic prednisone for rheumatoid arthritis presents with hypotension after surgery.
questions.question: What glucocorticoid doses suppress the HPA axis and require stress dosing?

options.text: HPA suppression only occurs with IV steroids
Only very high doses cause suppression
Inhaled steroids never cause suppression
Any dose >15-25mg hydrocortisone equivalent daily (>4-6mg prednisone, >0.5mg dexamethasone) for >3 weeks can suppress HPA axis and cause secondary AI

questions.explanation: HPA axis suppression can occur with: Prednisone ≥5mg/day (or equivalent) for ≥3 weeks. Risk increases with dose and duration. ANY route can cause suppression - oral, inhaled (high-dose), nasal, topical (potent, large area), intra-articular. Patients may develop secondary AI months after stopping steroids. Always assume HPA suppression in patients on chronic steroids who present acutely ill.

whyChain.why: How does this differ from primary AI?
whyChain.answer: Steroid-induced AI is SECONDARY (pituitary suppression). ACTH is low, and adrenal atrophy occurs. Key differences: (1) No hyperkalemia (aldosterone preserved via renin-angiotensin); (2) No hyperpigmentation (ACTH is low); (3) Hyponatremia can still occur. Treatment is the same: stress-dose hydrocortisone.

whyChain.why: How long does HPA suppression last?
whyChain.answer: Recovery time varies greatly - weeks to over a year. Gradual taper allows recovery. Patients should be assumed to have suppressed HPA axis until tested (morning cortisol >10 μg/dL or positive cosyntropin stimulation suggests recovery). Stress dosing should continue until recovery confirmed.

sources.text: StatPearls: Adrenal Crisis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: This patient's crisis might have been prevented with proper sick day management.
questions.question: What are the 'sick day rules' for patients with adrenal insufficiency?
questions.hint: Consider how to adjust steroids during illness.

options.text: Stop steroids during illness to avoid immunosuppression
Double oral dose for minor illness/fever >38°C; Triple dose for fever >39°C or significant illness; IM hydrocortisone if unable to take/absorb oral; seek emergency care if not improving
Only increase dose if hospitalized
Keep the same dose regardless of illness

questions.explanation: Sick day rules: (1) Minor illness/fever 38-39°C: DOUBLE usual oral hydrocortisone for 2-3 days; (2) Moderate illness/fever >39°C: TRIPLE dose; (3) Vomiting/unable to absorb oral: IM hydrocortisone 100mg immediately (patient should have kit at home); (4) Major stress (surgery, trauma): IV stress dosing (100mg bolus + 200mg/24h). ALL patients need education, emergency injection kit, medical alert identification, and steroid emergency card.

whyChain.why: Why doesn't this patient have an emergency injection kit?
whyChain.answer: This represents a failure of patient education and preparation. All AI patients should have: (1) Injectable hydrocortisone at home, (2) Training for self/family administration, (3) Medical alert bracelet/card, (4) Written sick day rules. Studies show many patients lack these resources despite guidelines.

Perioperative stress dosing: Minor surgery (local anesthesia): usual dose or double. Moderate surgery: 50mg IV hydrocortisone at induction + 25mg q8h x24h. Major surgery: 100mg at induction + 50mg q8h, taper over 2-3 days. Always communicate AI status to surgical/anesthesia team.

sources.text: StatPearls: Adrenal Crisis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: Integrating all information about this case.
questions.question: What is the complete diagnosis, treatment, and prevention plan?
questions.hint: Connect precipitant, pathophysiology, treatment, and prevention.

options.text: Adrenal crisis in known primary AI (Addison's + APS-2), precipitated by GI illness preventing oral medication absorption; immediate treatment: IV hydrocortisone 100mg + NS fluids + dextrose + hyperkalemia management; prevention: education on sick day rules + emergency injection kit + medical alert ID
Diabetic ketoacidosis
Hypothyroid crisis
Septic shock requiring antibiotics only

questions.explanation: Complete synthesis: (1) Diagnosis: Adrenal crisis in primary AI (APS-2 with Addison's, thyroiditis, T1DM); (2) Precipitant: GI illness → unable to absorb oral medications → inadequate cortisol during stress; (3) Presentation: classic (hypotension, hyponatremia, hyperkalemia, hypoglycemia, hyperpigmentation, eosinophilia); (4) Immediate treatment: IV hydrocortisone 100mg bolus → 200mg/24h, NS fluids, D50 for hypoglycemia, calcium gluconate for hyperkalemia; (5) Recovery: taper to oral hydrocortisone + fludrocortisone; (6) Prevention: education, sick day rules, IM injection kit, medical ID.

whyChain.answer: Once stable: (1) Resume oral hydrocortisone (15-25mg/day in divided doses) + fludrocortisone; (2) Ensure emergency supplies (injection kit, alert card); (3) Re-educate on sick day rules and when to self-inject; (4) Involve family in education; (5) Consider referral to support groups (Addison's Disease Self-Help Group); (6) Annual review of therapy and education.

mcqs.stem: Which of the following is the MOST important initial step in evaluating a patient with suspected adrenal crisis?

options.text: Obtain a thorough history and physical examination
options.explanation: A comprehensive history and physical examination is the foundation of clinical reasoning in adrenal crisis. It guides all subsequent diagnostic and management decisions.

options.text: Order advanced imaging immediately
options.explanation: Advanced imaging may be indicated but should be guided by clinical findings, not ordered reflexively.

options.text: Start empiric treatment before evaluation
options.explanation: Treatment should generally follow an appropriate diagnostic evaluation unless the patient is critically unstable.

options.text: Consult a specialist before any workup
options.explanation: Initial evaluation should be performed by the treating physician; specialist consultation follows based on findings.

mcqs.stem: A patient presents with classic features of adrenal crisis. Which clinical reasoning principle is MOST important in avoiding diagnostic error?

options.text: Search satisficing - stop looking after the first diagnosis
options.explanation: Premature closure (stopping the diagnostic process too early) is a common cognitive error that leads to missed diagnoses.

options.text: Consider the differential diagnosis systematically
options.explanation: Systematic consideration of differential diagnoses, including must-not-miss conditions, is essential for avoiding premature closure and anchoring bias.

options.text: Rely primarily on pattern recognition
options.explanation: While pattern recognition (System 1 thinking) is useful, it should be balanced with analytical reasoning (System 2) to avoid cognitive biases.

options.text: Order every available test to rule out all possibilities
options.explanation: Indiscriminate testing leads to false positives, unnecessary procedures, and increased cost without improving diagnostic accuracy.

mcqs.stem: When managing adrenal crisis, which approach best supports evidence-based practice?

options.text: Follow institutional protocols regardless of patient factors
options.explanation: Evidence-based practice integrates best evidence with clinical expertise AND patient values/circumstances.

options.text: Base decisions solely on clinical experience
options.explanation: Clinical experience is valuable but can introduce bias; it should be combined with current evidence.

options.text: Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values
options.explanation: Evidence-based medicine is the conscientious integration of best research evidence, clinical expertise, and patient values in making clinical decisions.

options.text: Always defer to the most recent published study
options.explanation: Individual studies should be appraised for quality and relevance; systematic reviews and guidelines generally provide stronger evidence.

mcqs.stem: A patient in DKA has a serum potassium of 3.1 mEq/L on presentation. What should be done BEFORE starting insulin?

options.text: Replace potassium to ≥3.3 mEq/L first, as insulin will further lower potassium and risk cardiac arrest
options.explanation: Per ADA guidelines, if K+ <3.3 mEq/L, potassium must be replaced BEFORE insulin administration. Insulin drives potassium intracellularly, and severe hypokalemia (K+ <3.0) can cause fatal cardiac arrhythmias. Replace with IV KCl 20-40 mEq/hr until K+ ≥3.3, then start insulin.

options.text: Start insulin immediately — DKA is life-threatening and cannot be delayed
options.explanation: While DKA requires urgent treatment, giving insulin with K+ <3.3 mEq/L can precipitate fatal hypokalemia. The 15-30 minute delay to partially correct potassium is critical for patient safety.

options.text: Give IV calcium gluconate for cardiac membrane stabilization, then start insulin
options.explanation: IV calcium stabilizes the myocardium in HYPERkalemia, not hypokalemia. For hypokalemia, direct potassium replacement is needed.

options.text: Start bicarbonate infusion to correct the acidosis, which will normalize potassium
options.explanation: Bicarbonate shifts potassium intracellularly and would WORSEN hypokalemia. Routine bicarbonate is not recommended in DKA unless pH <6.9.
